summary:
Nelson Mandela's presidency of South Africa from May 1994 to June 1999 was one of the most watched and symbolically significant political tenures of the twentieth century. Elected in South Africa's first democratic election following the end of [[event:2684|apartheid]], Mandela governed a country deeply divided by decades of racial segregation and violence with extraordinary grace and commitment to national reconciliation. His personal history — 27 years imprisoned on Robben Island — gave his calls for forgiveness and unity a moral authority that transcended politics.

Mandela's administration established the [[event:3490|Truth and Reconciliation Commission]] under Archbishop Desmond Tutu to address apartheid-era human rights violations, and oversaw the adoption of a new constitution in 1996 widely regarded as one of the most progressive in the world. Economically, the government pursued the Growth, Employment and Redistribution (GEAR) macroeconomic framework, which emphasized fiscal discipline and attracted criticism from labor unions and the left for not moving quickly enough on redistribution. The HIV/AIDS epidemic, which was devastating South Africa, was inadequately addressed under Mandela and more so under his successor Thabo Mbeki.

Perhaps Mandela's most symbolic act was his decision to serve only one term and retire voluntarily, demonstrating a commitment to democratic norms unusual among African leaders of his generation. His presidency established South Africa as a functioning multiracial democracy and a source of moral authority in international affairs, earning him virtually universal admiration worldwide.
